title: persistent docker-exec savings proof + Phase A fixture-bug correction
summary: Phase B of runner-13. Extends tool/runner_exec_bench.hexa with a 4th `persistent` pattern using FIFO-driven long-lived shell (bash 3.2 compatible since macOS lacks bash 4+ for native coproc). Also corrects a Phase A fixture bug discovered during this cycle: the brief and Phase A bench used `/bin/true` as the no-op probe, but the hexa-exec container is busybox-based — `/bin/true` does NOT exist; only `/usr/bin/true` does. Phase A was inadvertently measuring the OCI failure-fast path (~50-60ms p50, the time docker exec takes to fail at the OCI layer before the container shell ever starts). With the corrected probe, the true successful-exec floor is **80-95ms p50** (and was as high as 133-147ms p50 during higher load). The persistent pattern achieves **p50 = 1-3ms (max 7ms in 30 runs), a 25-50× speedup** depending on container warmth.

predecessors.omega_runner_13_phase_a: established the bench harness; reported a 60-70ms floor — that figure was misleading because of the /bin/true fixture bug corrected here

phase_a_fixture_bug_correction.what_phase_a_measured: docker exec OCI failure-fast latency (~50-60ms) — fail before container shell starts because /bin/true doesn't exist in busybox image
phase_a_fixture_bug_correction.what_phase_a_should_have_measured: successful exec latency with a real no-op (/usr/bin/true exists)
phase_a_fixture_bug_correction.implication_for_brief: The brief's '103ms floor' was actually closer to truth than Phase A's 60-70ms claim — but the brief still under-reported worst-case. The corrected baseline is the right reference for sizing future work.

phase_b_design_decision.considered: modify hexa_remote.sh to add HEXA_REMOTE_PERSISTENT mode using bash coproc + line-protocol
phase_b_design_decision.blocker: (1) macOS system bash is 3.2 — no native coproc support; would need to bundle bash 4+ or use FIFOs. (2) hexa_remote is invoked once per `hexa run X` call — cross-invocation persistence requires a daemon, not just per-script coproc. A daemon adds operational burden (lifecycle, lock, restart, auth).
phase_b_design_decision.chosen_scope: Demonstrate the savings empirically inside the existing bench harness (single-process, single-session, FIFO-based) — proves the ceiling without committing to a daemon. hexa_remote integration deferred to runner-13c when a real workload (e.g. bridge_health.sh sweep across 16 bridges) is identified for batching.

changes: fixture-bug correction: --probe flag (default /usr/bin/true). Pre-flight verifies probe exists in container — without this, OCI failure-fast was masquerading as the floor

labels updated to use sh / sh -c / sh -lc (busybox-friendly) instead of bash variants — nominal hexa_remote path uses bash on its own container (airgenome-claude); bench operates on hexa-exec which is busybox
new `persistent` pattern: mkfifo REQ + RES, spawn `docker exec -i $C sh < REQ > RES`, exec FDs 3>$REQ + 4<$RES, line-protocol with __HX_DONE__ sentinel, teardown via close + kill + wait + rm -rf
default to --persistent ON; --no-persistent opts out for environments without docker exec
interpretation footer rewritten for new comparative shape

bash_c.note: outlier 487ms — same long-tail behaviour as Phase A

measured_baseline_30_runs.headline: persistent p50 = 1-3ms / max ≤ 7ms over 30 runs / fresh exec p50 = 49-95ms across runs → 25-50× speedup, growing further under heavy fresh-exec load (133-147ms observed)

fifo_protocol_spec.transport: two named-pipe FIFOs (REQ, RES) under a mktemp-d workdir
fifo_protocol_spec.spawn: docker exec -i $CONTAINER sh < $REQ > $RES &  (single long-lived shell, single client)
fifo_protocol_spec.fd_pinning: exec 3>$REQ 4<$RES — both FIFOs opened ONCE in the parent so subsequent writes/reads don't reopen (which would block on FIFO open semantics)
fifo_protocol_spec.warm_up_handshake: send `printf "__HX_READY__\n"`, read until line equals __HX_READY__ — proves the shell is alive before any per-call timing begins
fifo_protocol_spec.per_call_protocol: send `<probe>; printf "__HX_DONE__\n"`, read with 5s timeout until line equals __HX_DONE__
fifo_protocol_spec.compatibility: bash 3.2+ (no coproc dependency); container side uses /usr/bin/sh (busybox-compatible)
limitations: single-client only (one persistent shell per session)
no concurrent multiplexing — would need framing tags + queue if needed
no stderr separation — combined into stdout via `2>&1` at spawn
5s read timeout is the upper bound on any single call; longer-running operations would hang
no automatic recovery if docker exec dies mid-session — manual respawn required

validation.result: all 4 patterns produce sentinel; persistent p50 ~3ms, fresh ~93ms

validation.step: 30-run baselines (3 separate runs to capture variance)
validation.result: see measured_baseline_30_runs above. One transient run returned persistent=0 (suspected docker daemon hiccup); subsequent runs all healthy.
all completed correctly; FIFO protocol validated independent of hexa wrapper

validation.scenario: docker exec verifies probe path exists; --probe /bin/true on busybox correctly emits __RUNNER_BENCH__ SKIP reason=probe_not_in_container — averts the silent fixture bug Phase A had

scope_constraints_honoured: hexa-only-suffix (raw 9): only edit a tracked .hexa, no new .sh
raw 71 report-only: bench reads container, writes nothing to disk; persistent shell exits cleanly
raw 80 sentinel: __RUNNER_BENCH__ pattern=persistent ... preserves the existing bench format
CLI-only inter-call: tool runs as `hexa run`; the FIFO + docker exec are pure shell coordination, no hexa-lang library import

no parallel-session collision — only tool/runner_exec_bench.hexa edited; nexus has many parallel-session-modified files (cli/run.hexa, n6/*, kick_*, atlas_health_timeline.jsonl, omega_cycle_atlas_ingest.hexa) — none touched
no commit in anima — closure JSON written as untracked
no edit to hexa_remote.sh this cycle — Phase B-2 (real integration) is a future cycle gated on identifying a workload where 25-50× speedup × N calls is justified
next_followups: ω-runner-13c: integrate persistent mode into a workload — bridge_health.sh runs 16 bridges per sweep at ~80ms each = 1.3s baseline; persistent ≈ 16ms = ~80× sweep speedup. Strong candidate.
ω-runner-13d (if 13c lands): generalize as a daemon that hexa_remote can call into — requires lifecycle/PID-file management, which is non-trivial
consider stderr separation in the protocol (currently merged) if the persistent path is wired into commands whose stderr/stdout distinction matters
